is the chief [tax](/wiki/Tax "Tax")-writing [committee](/wiki/List_of_current_United_States_House_of_Representatives_committees "List of current United States House of Representatives committees") of the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"). The committee has jurisdiction over all [taxation](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_States "Taxation in the United States"), [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff"), and other revenue-raising measures, as well as a number of other programs including [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)"), [unemployment benefits](/wiki/Unemployment_benefit "Unemployment benefit"), [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)"), the enforcement of [child support](/wiki/Child_support "Child support") laws, [Temporary Assistance for Needy Families](/wiki/Temporary_Assistance_for_Needy_Families "Temporary Assistance for Needy Families"), [foster care](/wiki/Foster_care "Foster care"), and [adoption](/wiki/Adoption "Adoption") programs. Members of the Ways and Means Committee are not allowed to serve on any other House committee unless they are granted a waiver from their party's congressional leadership. It has long been regarded as the most prestigious committee of the House of Representatives.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The **Committee on Ways and Means** is the chief [tax](/wiki/Tax "Tax")-writing [committee](/wiki/List_of_current_United_States_House_of_Representatives_committees "List of current United States House of Representatives committees") of the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"). The committee has jurisdiction over all [taxation](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_States "Taxation in the United States"), [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff"), and other revenue-raising measures, as well as a number of other programs including [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)"), [unemployment benefits](/wiki/Unemployment_benefit "Unemployment benefit"), [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)"), the enforcement of [child support](/wiki/Child_support "Child support") laws, [Temporary Assistance for Needy Families](/wiki/Temporary_Assistance_for_Needy_Families "Temporary Assistance for Needy Families"), [foster care](/wiki/Foster_care "Foster care"), and [adoption](/wiki/Adoption "Adoption") programs. Members of the Ways and Means Committee are not allowed to serve on any other House committee unless they are granted a waiver from their party's congressional leadership. It has long been regarded as the most prestigious committee of the House of Representatives.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") requires that all bills regarding taxation must originate in the U.S. House of Representatives, and House rules dictate that all bills regarding taxation must pass through Ways and Means. This system imparts upon the committee and its members a significant degree of influence over other representatives, committees, and public policy. Its Senate counterpart is the [U.S. Senate Committee on Finance](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_Finance "United States Senate Committee on Finance").

In the [119th Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), the committee's chair is [Jason T. Smith](/wiki/Jason_T._Smith "Jason T. Smith").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Recent chairs have included [Bill Thomas](/wiki/Bill_Thomas "Bill Thomas"), [Charlie Rangel](/wiki/Charlie_Rangel "Charlie Rangel"), [Sander Levin](/wiki/Sander_Levin "Sander Levin"), [Dave Camp](/wiki/Dave_Camp "Dave Camp"), [Paul Ryan](/wiki/Paul_Ryan "Paul Ryan"), [Kevin Brady](/wiki/Kevin_Brady "Kevin Brady"), and [Richard Neal](/wiki/Richard_Neal "Richard Neal").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_House_Committee_on_Ways_and_Means&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Ways_and_Means_2020.jpg/250px-Ways_and_Means_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ways_and_Means_2020.jpg)

Inside a Ways and Means meeting in November 2019

The Ways and Means Committee was first established during the first Congress, in 1789. However, this initial version was disbanded after only 8 weeks; for the next several years, only ad hoc committees were formed, to write up laws on notions already debated in the whole House. It was first established as a standing committee by resolution adopted December 21, 1795,[[3]](#cite_note-3) and first appeared among the list of regular standing committees on January 7, 1802.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Upon its original creation, it held power over both taxes and spending, until the spending power was given to the new [Appropriations Committee](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Appropriations "United States House Committee on Appropriations") in 1865.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

During the Civil War the key policy-maker in Congress was [Thaddeus Stevens](/wiki/Thaddeus_Stevens "Thaddeus Stevens"), as chair of the committee and Republican floor leader. He took charge of major legislation that funded the war effort and permanently transformed the nation's economic policies regarding tariffs, bonds, income and excise taxes, national banks, suppression of money issued by state banks, greenback currency, and western railroad land grants.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Stevens was one of the major policymakers regarding Reconstruction, and obtained a House vote of impeachment against President [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") (who was acquitted by the Senate in 1868). [Hans L. Trefousse](/wiki/Hans_L._Trefousse "Hans L. Trefousse"), his leading biographer, concludes that Stevens "was one of the most influential representatives ever to serve in Congress. [He dominated] the House with his wit, knowledge of parliamentary law, and sheer willpower, even though he was often unable to prevail."[[7]](#cite_note-7) Historiographical views of Stevens have dramatically shifted over the years, from the early 20th-century view of Stevens and the [Radical Republicans](/wiki/Radical_Republicans "Radical Republicans") as tools of enormous business and motivated by hatred of the white South, to the perspective of the [neoabolitionists](/wiki/Neoabolitionism_(race_relations) "Neoabolitionism (race relations)") of the 1950s and afterwards, who applauded their efforts to give equal rights to the freed slaves.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Three future presidents – [James Polk](/wiki/James_Polk "James Polk"), [Millard Fillmore](/wiki/Millard_Fillmore "Millard Fillmore"), and [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley") – served as committee chair. Before the official roles of [floor leader](/wiki/Floor_leader "Floor leader") came about in the late 19th century, the chair of Ways and Means was considered the majority leader. The chair is one of very few representatives to have office space within the Capitol building itself.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Because of its wide jurisdiction, Ways and Means has always been one of the most important committees with respect to impact on policy. Although it lacks the prospects for reelection help that comes with the [Appropriations Committee](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Appropriations "United States House Committee on Appropriations"), it is seen as a valuable post for two reasons: given the wide array of interests that are affected by the committee, a seat makes it easy to collect [campaign contributions](/wiki/Campaign_finance "Campaign finance")[[9]](#cite_note-9) and since its range is broad, members with a wide array of policy concerns often seek positions to be able to influence policy decisions. Some recent major issues that have gone through the Ways and Means Committee include [welfare reform](/wiki/Welfare_reform "Welfare reform"), a Medicare [prescription drug](/wiki/Prescription_drug "Prescription drug") benefit, [Social Security reform](/wiki/Social_Security_reform "Social Security reform"), [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush")'s tax cuts, and trade agreements including the [North American Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/North_American_Free_Trade_Agreement "North American Free Trade Agreement") (NAFTA) and the [Central America Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Central_America_Free_Trade_Agreement "Central America Free Trade Agreement") (CAFTA).

From 1911 to 1974, the Ways and Means Committee also had the responsibility to appoint members of other committees in addition to its legislative duties.[[10]](#cite_note-'70s_276-10) When Ways and Means chair [Wilbur Mills](/wiki/Wilbur_Mills_(politician) "Wilbur Mills (politician)")' career ended in scandal, Congressman [Phillip Burton](/wiki/Phillip_Burton "Phillip Burton") transferred the committee's selection powers to a separate, newly created committee.[[10]](#cite_note-'70s_276-10)

## Subcommittees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_House_Committee_on_Ways_and_Means&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Subcommittees")]

There are six subcommittees in the 118th Congress. In 2011, the [Subcommittee on Income Security and Family Support](/wiki/United_States_House_Ways_and_Means_Subcommittee_on_Income_Security_and_Family_Support "United States House Ways and Means Subcommittee on Income Security and Family Support") was renamed the Subcommittee on Human Resources, returning to the name it held prior to the [110th United States Congress](/wiki/110th_United_States_Congress "110th United States Congress").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2015, the [Select Revenue Measures](/wiki/United_States_House_Ways_and_Means_Subcommittee_on_Select_Revenue_Measures "United States House Ways and Means Subcommittee on Select Revenue Measures") was renamed the Subcommittee on Tax Policy.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2019 these two subcommittees were again renamed under Democratic control; Human Resources became Worker and Family Support and Tax Policy was renamed to Select Revenue Measures. In 2023 and under a return to Republican control, they were again renamed to Work and Welfare and Tax respectively.
